content.full_text:
SCHOOLS LEGAL SERVICE

FRANK J. FEKETE
GENERAL COUNSEL

PETER C. CARTON
STEPHEN L. HARTSELL
DWAINE L. CHAMBERS
MAILING ADDRESS: P.O. BOX 11110
5801 SUNDALE AVENUE
BAKERSFIELD, CALIFORNIA 93389
(805) 396-3830
FAX 398-3843

FRANK W. RONICH
MELVIN D. PETERS
COUNSEL

CARL B.A. LANGE III
DIRECTOR OF LABOR RELATIONS

PHIL LANCASTER
ANTHONY V. LEONIS
BARGAINING SPECIALIST

February 22, 1990


Kathy Donovan, Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Re:  Request for Opinion; Elected Official Asserting
     A Dual Role as Trustee and Employee

Dear Ms. Donovan:

We represent the San Luis Coastal Unified School District.  Board
member Caroline Botwin ("Botwin"), a newly-elected trustee, wants
to serve the district both on the governing board and as a paid
employee (teacher).

The board has asked us to investigate the obvious conflict of
interest issues raised by this dual-role status.  We are working
with the Office of the Attorney General on issues presented by
Government Code Section 1090, et seq., Government Code Section
1126, and the common law of conflicts of interest and incompatible
activities.  At the same time, we are contacting your office with
respect to conflict issues arising under the Political Reform Act
of 1974 (Government Code Sections 81000, et seq.), which we call
the "Act" in this letter.

We talked with one of your staff attorneys last week to get some
general procedural information.  Beyond confirming some general
observations about how the Act and your Agency work, she suggested
that we put the district's questions in writing.  We understand
that your Agency responds to opinion requests such as ours within
25 days.

The questions arise out of the following fact situation.

FACTS PRESENTED

A district employee, Botwin, is elected to the governing board at
the November 1989 election.  Upon assuming the public office of
trustee, Botwin declines to resign the employment role.  Instead,
Botwin asserts a right to remain a paid employee while
simultaneously sitting as a trustee.


Kathy Donovan, Chief Counsel
February 22, 1990
Page 2


Botwin is a member of a the district's certificated employee
collective bargaining unit. The bargaining agreement between the
district governing board and the unit covers many topics, including
discipline, reassignment, layoff and compensation of unit members.

At the same time, board members -- including Botwin -- would
regularly be asked to consider agenda items involving matters such
as discipline, reassignment, layoff and compensation of Botwin and
other unit members. Each budget augmentation and expenditure item
and decision necessarily affects the amount of money available for
compensation of employees, as determined by collective bargaining.

Each board agenda will include matters in which Botwin has an
interest, including matters such as -

     1.  Nonreelection under Education Code Section 44929.21 of
certificated employees.

     2.  Compensation for classified staff (in the bargaining
unit).

     3.  Purchase of a portable building (as a representative
purchaser of goods and services).

     4.  Monthly certificated salary warrant approval for Botwin
and for other employees under Education Code Section 45055.

     5.  Consulting with and instructing the board's negotiator as
to changes to the collective bargaining agreements for certificated
and classified staff.

                         PRELIMINARY OBSERVATIONS

Your staff attorney agreed with us about certain preliminary
observations.

     1.  The Act does not allow your Agency to review or pass on
conflict of interest issues arising under Government Code Sections
1090, et seq., Government Code Section 1126 and the common law of
conflicts of interest and incompatible activities. You routinely
refer those matters back to the public entity's counsel or suggest
a consultation with the Attorney General's Office.

     2.  Public entity employment earnings are apparently exempt
from the reporting portion of the Act's prohibitions against having

Kathy Donovan, Chief Counsel
February 22, 1990
Page 3


a financial interest. Government Code Section 82030, subdivision
(b)(2), definitionally exempts public entity income from the
financial interest disclosure requirements of Government Code
Sections 87100 and 87103. But the Act does not exempt an
official's interest in a business entity from the basic
prohibitions against conflict of interest in Sections 87100, et
seq.

     3. While the district is apparently not a business entity
under Section 82005, you have never opined as to whether an
employee union or bargaining unit association is a business entity
within that definition.

     4. Those persons drafting the Title 2 regulations apparently
assumed that no elected board member might ever assert a right to
serve in a dual capacity simultaneously as an employee and an
employee of the same public entity. Actually, that reconfirms the
board's perception of what has always been the law in this state.
A cursory examination of the history of similar posts, such as county
supervisor or city council member, confirms the statewide
contemporaneous administrative construction that a dual capacity
situation is unlawful and impossible.

QUESTIONS PRESENTED

     With the above in mind, the following are the district's initial
questions under the Act's conflict of interest sections (Government
Code Sections 87100-87313).

     1. Is Botwin's certificated bargaining unit a business entity
within the meaning of Government Code Section 82005?

     2. Please tell us the extent, if any, to which the following
are activities from which Botwin is disqualified under the Act:

          A. Participating in board deliberations or voting on
whether to give an Education Code Section 44929.21 notice of
nonreelection to a fellow certificated unit member.

          B. Participating in board deliberations or voting on
whether to implement incentive pay or any other changes in
compensation for classified staff.

          C. Participating in board deliberations or voting on a
proposal to expend district funds for a portable building or
other goods and services).

Kathy Donovan, Chief Counsel
February 22, 1990
Page 4


D. Participating in board deliberations or voting under
Education Code Section 45055 on whether to approve salary
warrants for Botwin or for others in the bargaining unit.

E. Participating in or voting on any matter arising
under or affecting collective bargaining for any employee
group or unit.

OUR PRELIMINARY CONCLUSIONS

We have conducted a preliminary analysis of the Act and the
subsequently-adopted Title 8 regulations. Here are our
observations and conclusions:

1. Botwin's certificated bargaining unit is a recognized
exclusive representative under the Educational Employment Relations
Act (Government Code Sections 3540, et seq.) As such, the unit is
a business entity within the meaning of Government Code Section
87103, subdivisions (a) and (d) and Title 2 regulation section
18700.1, subdivision (a). At a minimum, Botwin's relationship and
membership in the unit requires (a) reporting the existence of the
interest and (b) [under Title 2 regulation section 18702.1,
subdivision (a)(4)] disqualification on all matters relating to
Botwin's unit.

2. When voting, Botwin "makes a governmental decision" under
Government Code Section 87100. This seems confirmed by Title 2
regulation Section 18700, subdivision (b)(1).

3. When joining in board deliberations, Botwin participates
in the "making of a governmental decision" under Government Code
Section 87100. This seems confirmed by Title 2 regulation Section
18700, subdivision (c).

4. To the extent that Title 2 regulation Section 18700
subdivision (d) might appear to exempt a board member from the
Act's proscriptions against decision-making or participation in
decisions on Botwin's relationship to the bargaining unit, the
regulation appears to be ambiguous and in conflict with the purpose
of the statute and requires clarification when applied to these
facts. This regulatory exemption does not appear to have addressed
- or been intended to exempt - a board member's action on his or
her own bargaining unit contract.

Kathy Donovan, Chief Counsel
February 22, 1990
Page 5


     5.  Similarly, the apparent exemption language in Section
Title 2 regulation Section 18700.1, subdivision (b)(3), absolving
attempts to influence others when an official "negotiates his or
her compensation or the terms and conditions of his or her
employment or contract" appears to conflict with the purpose of the
statute and seems unlawful as applied to these facts.  This
exemption does not appear to have addressed - or been intended to
exempt - a board member's action on his or her own bargaining unit
contract.

     6.  Each of the board decisions referred to in the typical
agenda items above requires the exercise of discretion rather than
being solely "ministerial, secretarial, manual or clerical" under
regulation Section 18700, subdivision (d)(1).

     7.  Botwin's interest in each transaction by the governing
board affecting her bargaining unit disqualifies Botwin from
participation or voting under penalty of civil and/or criminal
proceedings under Government Code Sections 91000, et seq. We must
therefore conclude that the Act disqualifies Botwin from
participating or voting on any of the agenda items listed above.
And if Botwin attempts to vote, that vote must not be considered
or counted.

                         A CONCLUDING NOTE

     Even if your Agency might disagree with one or more of our
preliminary conclusions as to conflicts arising under the Act, your
opinion will not address the separate conflict of interest
prohibitions under Government Code Sections 1090, et seq.,
Government Code Section 1126 and the common law of conflicts of
interest and incompatible activities. For instance, the Act under
which your Agency operates does not provide for invalidation of a
entire board vote to approve a matter in which Botwin has a Section
1090 interest.  Government Code Sections 1090, et seq., on the
other hand, provide additional authority to void the entire
transaction.  See Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal 3d 633, 214 Cal.
Rptr. 139.

     Your reply should be directed to this office.  Please call if you
need any additional information.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

PETER C. CARTON

PCC:ep
